item.title: right up there with Grammie (SCP-517, "Grammie Knows")
item.content:encoded:
I neglected to comment on this when I first read it, but now that I'm here, I've got to say: I absolutely love this! I don't know how much of it is due to being an adamant technophile, or if it's just because of how empathetic you made Jamie, but this is one of my top 2, along with (as mentioned) SCP-517. Well done!
Edit: I feel dumb, but at least not as dumb as I was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the ending, so funny.

Here's some art of it, incredibly creative SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A SCP so good it plugs malware at you. I'm joking, though wow, that is something I should consider if I ever make up fake urls. People can be just the worst types of people.

I liked the slow progression of JaJa to a more aggressive programmer who was willing to launch campaigns to hurt or kill individuals who stomped on his creativity. From a guy who just wanted to pal-around with ideas.

Well, it took a little over a year, but we're here! I have not been as excited for a +100 in a long, long time. This is one of few things I have written where I felt like it really deserved +100. Like, I had put the effort in, I had really revised and refined it, it was a good product. Other things, sure, +100, good for them, feels good. This feels like a real, solid achievement. Thanks y'all for reading. Perhaps JaJa2020 will appear again :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super sorry if I gave you malware, didn't mean to, the links have been fixed.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I mean, it's a perfectly reasonable mistake to make — it never even occurred to me when I was writing fake email addresses that those fake email addresses might actually one day resolve to real ones!

Shaggy already shifted the links to a fake page; I would tentatively recommend changing the URLs (and email addresses) to either contain black boxes (invalidating them) or point to reserved DNS addresses (such as ".net" — you can mix it up, though — there's a list available of reserved extensions here).

(I'm not staff, obviously; I'm only recommending this in my capacity as another user/writer)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Oh jeez, I’m really sorry about this. I thought these would just lead to random places and didn’t give it a second thought. Sorry to cause this trouble.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
So, I talked to bluesoul about this in IRC chat, and he pointed out that there's a lot of reserved extensions you can use for fictitious websites/email addresses that will pretty much never resolve to anything (or if they do, they'll resolve to addresses that are tightly controlled and not likely to redirect maliciously or anything). One example he gave is ".int" — so a solution might be to just replace the extension ".com" to ".int" throughout this article.

(This is somewhat akin to movies using 555-#### for a phone-number to avoid accidentally broadcasting someone's actual phone-number)

I think this might be a good overall policy to broadcast to authors, somewhere — not as a rule, but as a guideline, possibly on one of the template pages. Use ".int" or choose from a list of similar extensions for fictitious emails and URLs, so we avoid this problem in the future. But that's part of a broader discussion that probably doesn't belong in this specific thread.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting here that I edited the urls on this page to redirect to a nonexistent page on the wiki to avoid users potentially accessing dangerous/sensitive content.

Users are advised to be conscientious when including full urls on their pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: why the fuck is your websites maliciously redirecting me my man. I loved the scip and clicked the site to see what might be on it and Norton shits a brick. what's the deal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Always love seeing the Foundation's policy of aggressive entitlement come around and bite them in the ass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great work. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as the end was funny, I actually disliked the behavior of the Foundation on this one, in several places.

The compliance of JaJa with the Foundation, the history of betrayal from other user, those where good readings.

Operation Aggressive Networking, though, is completely off for the Foundation to me. I'm not a fan of SCP weaponization, and I hardly see the Foundation putting this much in use of an anomaly without even testing it properly.

It is also very unlikely that they would do so messily. They are conducting huge military expeditions in front of civilians that could be spying on then, is that serious?

Maybe this could work better with involvement of other GoI with more aggressive behavior, like GOC or CI, but as a Foundation operation it seemed too off for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, the ending perfectly closes the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this. I really sympathized with the POI throughout, and felt bad that they'd gotten taken advantage of so badly until I saw the paywall at the end. Perfect ending. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fan-Fucking-Tastic!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weeeeeeird duuuude

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no words to properly express how happy this comment makes me, thank you~!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is properly spectacular. There's a lot of different things going on here, and your dedication to the contrasting natures of the Foundation's cold transcription of events with Jamie's humanness and warmth (particularly as you learn more about their effort and intents) did so so much to make it all shine. This is going on my list of skips I show people to get them into the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have Captain Kirby to thank for the paywall cockblock on Jamie's part. He also was the one that helped me trim the wording down by a whopping 10%. So yay!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DarkStuff (and Billith, it seems), you've done it again. Fun to read, nice to think about, surprisingly easy to empathise with and just generally a good, well-rounded piece of fiction. +1

Also literal paywalls jesus christ.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to read through this twice to grasp the concept, but it was worth it. I was genuinely sympathetic with the PoI at the end - all that work to make something cool, and here comes the Foundation tracking mud all over his nice clean floor.

item.title: Re: AAAAAAAAAAAAAA
item.content:encoded: hey, I'll take co-author credit any day. Base idea was yours, I'm sure my knowhow only stood on the shoulders of giants.

item.title: AAAAAAAAAAAAAA
item.content:encoded:
A SERIES II SLOT

AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA

Super special thanks to Captain Kirby, Varaxous, and especially Billith for looking this over. Specifically, Billith did like 90% of the technical jargon that makes it sound cool. If he asked this to be considered co-authored, I would make it so in a heart beat, because he practically rewrote the description and containment procedures. (Open invitation, Billith)

This was really fun to write! It clocks under 3,000 words, so despite having a four part addendum, it still meets my head requirements for a good short article. The containment procedures and the description both fit fully on screen at the same time, so I am really happy about that. I intentionally made no mention of GAW, though you may choose to headcanon this however you choose. I also intentionally made Jamie gender neutral, so you may headcanon that as well. Hopefully this is a compelling enough story. Cheers!

Now that it is entirely co-authored, the credits:
Billith — Wrote the containment procedures, and the description, did tons of small edits.
DarkStuff — Wrote the story and the original drafts, addenda remain mostly of his design (though as has been stated, Billith did tons of small edits)

Oh, hey, almost missed this.
Author art!
